title: SSH Password Spray
briefing: Authentication alerts on $TARGET show one external source trying a spread of different usernames against SSH, only a few attempts each — a low-and-slow spray designed to stay under lockout thresholds rather than hammer one account. Determine the source, what makes this different from a classic brute force, and how to respond.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 5710. Look at the DECODED user in each event (data.srcuser / the log message): many DIFFERENT usernames, few tries each, from a single srcip. That pattern is password spraying.

questions.explain: A single external IP ($SRC1) cycled through many usernames.

options: Password spraying (many users, few attempts each)
Single-account brute force (one user, many attempts)
questions.answer: Password spraying (many users, few attempts each)
questions.explain: One source, many different usernames, few attempts per account, spaced out — classic spray to dodge lockout.
agent.name on the auth alerts is $TARGET.

questions.prompt: Most specific MITRE ATT&CK technique ID?
questions.explain: T1110.003 Password Spraying (sub-technique of T1110 Brute Force).
Spraying is a real threat but slow and unconfirmed — medium, raise if any login succeeds.

options: Check whether ANY sprayed account actually authenticated
Nothing — few attempts per account is safe
answer: Check whether ANY sprayed account actually authenticated
questions.explain: Block, hunt for a success, add MFA, tune lockout. 'Few attempts is safe' is the trap — spraying is designed to look safe.
